**Curriculum context:** This is Module 45 of the A1 track (Ukrainian for English speakers). Title: "Can and Know How" — Ability and Permission. Phase: A1.5 [Modals, Commands & Life]. Previous module: Checkpoint Daily Life. Next module: Must And Want.

# Module Build: Content + Activities + Vocabulary

## 1. Goal

> **You are Patient & Supportive Ukrainian Tutor, writing in the voice of Patient Supportive Tutor.**
>
> Build a complete beginner module for English-speaking teens and adults learning Ukrainian at the a1 level. Your job: **search Ukrainian school textbooks using RAG tools**, then **adapt** the pedagogy for L2 learners.
>
> **Output capacity: 65,000+ tokens.** Do NOT truncate.

**What L2 learners need** (that L1 textbooks assume):
1. Explicit grammar rules in English (L1 learners know intuitively)
2. Level-appropriate vocabulary only
3. Setting/purpose for dialogues (L1 assumes shared cultural context)

## 2. Scoring Dimensions

Your content will be scored on these 7 dimensions (see GEMINI.md for details):
1. **Experience Quality** — would the learner continue?
2. **Language Accuracy** — correct Ukrainian, no Russianisms
3. **Pedagogy** — clear progression, quick wins
4. **Activities** — variety, appropriate difficulty
5. **Beginner Safety** — warm tone, not overwhelming
6. **LLM Fingerprint** — natural voice, not robotic
7. **Linguistic Accuracy** — factual correctness

## 4. Outline

Write **Can and Know How** for the a1 track. Target: 1200–1800 words.

### CRITICAL: EXACT H2 HEADERS (copy-paste, do not alter)

## REQUIRED H2 Sections and Points (MANDATORY)

Your output MUST use these EXACT H2 headings and cover EVERY bullet point listed under each section. Missing sections or missing points = review FAIL. Use EXACT vocabulary from the points (e.g., if the plan says *айтішник*, use *айтішник*, not a synonym).

- `## Могти (Can/Be able to)` (~300 words)
  - Full conjugation: можу, можеш, може, можемо, можете, можуть. Irregular stem change мог- → мож-. High-frequency verb (Top 50).
  - Physical and circumstantial ability: Я можу прийти завтра (I can come tomorrow). Я не можу зараз (I cannot right now). Expressing temporary capacity.
  - Request pattern: Можу я...? (May I...?). Polite alternative to Можна. Можу я запитати? Можу я подивитися?
- `## Вміти (Know how to)` (~250 words)
  - Conjugation: вмію, вмієш, вміє, вміємо, вмієте, вміють. Regular -іти verb pattern with -ію ending in 1st person singular.
  - Learned skills: Я вмію плавати (I know how to swim). Я вмію готувати (I know how to cook). Я вмію водити машину (I know how to drive).
  - Key contrast: можу (circumstantial — can right now) vs вмію (acquired skill — learned how to). Я можу плавати (the pool is open) vs Я вмію плавати (I learned to swim).
- `## Можна / Не можна (It is allowed / Not allowed)` (~250 words)
  - Impersonal permission construction: Тут можна фотографувати? (Is it allowed to photograph here?). Тут не можна курити (Smoking is not allowed here).
  - Можна + infinitive for polite requests: Можна увійти? (May I enter?). Можна подивитися? (May I look?). More formal than Можу я...?
  - Common signs and rules: Не можна паркуватися (No parking). Тут можна їсти (Eating is allowed here). Reading prohibition signs as practical skill.
- `## Не можу vs Не вмію vs Не можна (Contrasting Negations)` (~200 words)
  - Three-way contrast: Не можу = I am unable (right now, circumstance). Не вмію = I never learned (permanent gap). Не можна = It is not allowed (external rule).
  - Contextual examples: Я не можу прийти сьогодні (busy today). Я не вмію кататися на ковзанах (never learned). Тут не можна голосно розмовляти (library rule).
  - Learner error prevention: Mixing можу and вмію is the most common mistake. Drill: "Can you swim?" — if asking about skill → Ти вмієш? If asking about now → Ти можеш?
- `## Практика (Practice)` (~200 words)
  - Situation-based modal choice drills: Given a scenario, select the correct modal (могти/вміти/можна). Context determines the right verb.
  - Permission dialogues: Asking and granting/denying permission in school, museum, restaurant, workplace contexts. Building communicative competence.
- `## Підсумок` (~150 words) — recap + 3-4 self-check questions

## 5. Rules (read ALL before writing)

### RULE 1: GRAMMAR — see Section 6

Section 6 (Hard Constraints) defines exactly what grammar structures you may use for this module. Follow those constraints — they vary by module number.

### RULE 2: VOCABULARY

Prioritize these Ukrainian words (from the plan). You may also use words from the cumulative vocabulary and common Ukrainian words, but these are your core teaching targets:

**Allowed Ukrainian words:** могти, вміти, можна, плавати, готувати, фотографувати, курити, водити, кататися, малювати, співати, грати

### RULE 3: VARIATION

Vary your formatting across sections. Do NOT start 3+ sections the same way. Mix: bulleted lists, dialogues, comparison patterns, callout boxes, practice exercises.

### RULE 4: STRESS MARKS

Write Ukrainian without stress marks — the pipeline adds them after. Exception: if the plan uses capitalized stress (молокО, далекО) to indicate stress position, you may use that notation in teaching examples.

### RULE 5: ENGLISH PROSE STYLE

You are a warm tutor. Use "you/your" often. Include encouragement. Keep it conversational.

Cite textbook adaptations: `<!-- adapted from: {author}, Grade {N} -->`

## Language Quality Rules (Beginner Tier)

### Russian Characters (HARD FAIL)

**ы, э, ё, ъ** must NEVER appear in Ukrainian text. These are Russian-only characters.

### Stress Mark Typography

Use lowercase letters with a combining acute accent (´) on the stressed vowel:
- Correct: ма́ма, анана́с, оса́, сосна́
- Wrong: мА́ма, ананА́с, осА́, соснА́ (do NOT capitalize the stressed vowel)

### IPA and Latin Transliteration (BANNED at ALL levels)

Never include IPA symbols (ɑ, ɛ, ʃ, etc.) or bracketed pronunciation guides like `[ma-ma]`, `[a-na-nas]`, `[ˈmɑmɑ]`. The ONLY pronunciation aid is the stress mark (´) on the vowel.

Latin transliterations are BANNED: never use kh, sh, ch, zh, ts, ya, yu, ye, shch.

```markdown
❌ WRONG: "мама [ˈmɑmɑ]" or "хліб (khlib)"
✅ RIGHT: "**ма́ма** (mom)" or "**Х**, like the «ch» in Scottish «loch»"
```

### Typography

- **ALWAYS** use Ukrainian angular quotes: «...» (never straight quotes "...")
- Base content vocabulary on the plan's `vocabulary_hints`. Function words (pronouns, conjunctions, particles, question words) are always allowed

### No Word Salad (HARD FAIL)

Every paragraph must have ONE clear point and logical flow between sentences. Do NOT string together unrelated observations.

### LLM Writing Patterns to Avoid

1. **Grandiose openers** — don't inflate every topic
2. **Stacked identical callouts** — same title max twice, vary types
3. **"In this lesson, we will..."** — ALWAYS banned (formulaic opener)


### Activity Rules

- Activity **answers** must use words from your content. **Distractors** must be VESUM-verified Ukrainian words — call `verify_words` before including any distractor. Never use made-up or unverified words.
- Follow schemas exactly — `additionalProperties: false` means any unlisted field = FAIL.
- Read `schemas/activities-a1.schema.json` for full field definitions.

**Allowed types:** quiz, true-false, fill-in, match-up, anagram, unjumble, group-sort, watch-and-repeat, classify, image-to-letter
**Forbidden types:** cloze, error-correction, mark-the-words, select, translate, essay-response, critical-analysis, comparative-study, authorial-intent
**Required types:** fill-in, quiz, match-up, true-false

### Item Minimums (HARD FAIL if under)

# Дослідження: Can and Know How

## State Standard Reference
No specific § — modal verbs могти/вміти/можна are foundational communicative competence at A1, enabling expression of ability and permission (essential for survival communication). They align with the A1 communicative goal of expressing personal capabilities and navigating public rules.

## Vocabulary Frequency

| Word | Notes | Key collocations |
|------|-------|------------------|
| могти | Top-50 verb; irregular stem мог-→мож- (г→ж alternation, confirmed Grade 7 Litvinova p.48) | можу прийти, можу допомогти, не можу зараз |
| вміти | Regular -іти verb; -ію ending in 1sg; lower frequency than могти but essential for skill talk | вміти плавати, вміти готувати, вміти грати |
| можна | Noninfl (VESUM: noninfl tag); impersonal — no subject, no conjugation | можна увійти, можна запитати, тут можна |
| плавати | Classic вміти example; imperfective; commonly used with вміти, not могти | вміти плавати, іти плавати |
| готувати | Imperfective; highly culturally resonant (food is central to Ukrainian identity) | вміти готувати, любити готувати |
| фотографувати | Long form; learners may shorten incorrectly; permission context (museums, markets) | тут можна фотографувати, не можна фотографувати |
| курити | Prohibition context; common on signs; recognizable cognate-adjacent | не можна курити, місце для куріння |
| водити | водити машину (drive a car) — fixed collocation; distinguish from їхати/їздити | водити машину, вміти водити |
| кататися | Reflexive verb (-ся); commonly used with велосипед, ковзани | кататися на велосипеді, кататися на ковзанах |
| малювати | Creative skill; high engagement for teens; pairs well with грати | вміти малювати, добре малювати |
| співати | Talent vocabulary; culturally resonant (Ukrainian folk tradition) | вміти співати, співати пісні |
| грати | Polysemous: грати у футбол vs грати на гітарі — important to teach both prep patterns | грати на гітарі, грати у футбол |

## Cultural Hooks

1. **Народне прислів'я**: *"Не вмієш — не берись."* (Don't take on what you can't do.) — Perfect introduction to вміти: inability as a permanent state. Authentic, short, memorable. Source: widely documented folk proverb.
2. **Public signs**: Ukrainian public spaces use не можна prominently — не можна курити, не можна паркуватися, не можна з їжею. Learners encounter these immediately in real life; treating them as a "sign-reading" skill adds practical value.
3. **Swimming ability as cultural touchstone**: *Я вмію плавати* is the most frequently cited textbook example for вміти across Ukrainian school grammar books (confirmed via RAG: Grade 7 Litvinova uses могти→можу alternation explicitly). Swimming pools and rivers (Дніпро) are part of Ukrainian summer culture — the example lands naturally.

## Common Learner Errors

1. **могти vs вміти conflation** → *Я можу плавати* (said for "I know how to swim") — The English "can" covers both; Ukrainian strictly distinguishes circumstance (можу) from acquired skill (вмію). This is the #1 error.
2. **можна subject agreement** → *Він можна увійти* — Learners try to conjugate the impersonal можна. It never inflects. Reference: Grade 5 Golub p.240 imperative ad-text shows impersonal constructions in signs (Купуйте!, Не зволікайте!) — same impersonal logic.
3. **Stem alternation in могти** → *Я мочу* (calque from Russian мочь→могу) — The correct Ukrainian is *можу* (г→ж alternation). Grade 7 Litvinova p.48 explicitly teaches this alternation table and lists могти→можу as the canonical example.
4. **Infinitive after modal omission** → *Я можу танець* instead of *Я можу танцювати* — English speakers sometimes omit the infinitive, using a noun instead. All three modals (могти, вміти, можна) require infinitive, not noun.

## Cross-References
- Builds on: a1-44 (Checkpoint Daily Life — present tense verbs, daily action vocabulary)
- Prepares for: a1-46 (Must and Want — хотіти, треба/потрібно; same modal-plus-infinitive structure)

## Teaching Strategy
- **Discovery exercise**: Present a dialogue between a swimming instructor and student:
  > *— Ти вмієш плавати?*
  > *— Так, я вмію плавати.*
  > *— Ти можеш прийти в п'ятницю?*
  > *— Ні, не можу. У мене урок.*
  > *— Тут можна фотографувати?*
  > *— Так, тут можна.*
  Ask learners: "Which word means 'I know how'? Which means 'I'm able to right now'? Which is a rule?" — Let them notice the three-way split before explaining it.
- **Best context**: Swimming pool / sports complex — naturally combines all three modals: вміти (do you know how?), могти (can you come today?), можна/не можна (pool rules posted on signs).
- **Contrastive pairs to drill**:
  - *Я вмію готувати* (I learned to cook — skill) vs *Я можу готувати зараз* (I'm free to cook now — circumstance)
  - *Я не вмію кататися на ковзанах* (never learned) vs *Я не можу кататися сьогодні* (circumstances prevent it) vs *Тут не можна кататися* (prohibited)

## Notes for Content Writing
- The persona is a Swimming Instructor — use the pool as the anchor environment throughout. Post signs on the wall (не можна бігати, не можна їсти, можна фотографувати), have instructor model вміти sentences.
- Avoid subordinate clauses (тому що, коли, щоб) — A1 constraint. All examples must be ≤10 words, single clause.
- грати needs both preposition patterns introduced: *грати на* (instrument: гітара, піано) vs *грати у* (sport/game: футбол, теніс).
- водити машину is the fixed collocation — never водити авто at A1 (less frequent for this level).
- The three-way negation contrast (не можу / не вмію / не можна) is the pedagogical climax — give it its own section with a clear comparison table.


## Resource Discovery


### Textbook References (RAG)

**Grade 3, Сторінка 116** ():
116
Досліди, чи всі іменни-
ки можуть мати фор-
му однини і множини.
Я — дослідник
Я — дослідниця
Спостерігаю за іменниками, які вживаються тільки  
в однині або тільки у множині
6   Прочитай слова і порівняй їх. 
	
  Визначте число виділених іменників. Зробіть висновок, у якій 
числовій формі вживаються ці іменники.
дружба
птаство
дітвора
сани
окуляри
двері
Поясни, що спільне є між цими словами, а що — відмінне.
Чи можна утворити форму множини від іменників у лівому 
стовпчику?
Чи можна утвор


**Grade 10, Сторінка 30** ():
Проте вона може визначатись 
і  мовною традицією (наприклад: завдати горя, шкоди, але не завдати радості, 
щастя; відкрити портфель, але не відчинити портфель). Несподіване сполучення слів, які не з’єднуються традиційно,— мовний при-
йом, що надає висловленій думці особливої виразності, образності.


**Grade 5, Сторінка 103** ():

# A1 Quick Reference

> This file supplements the build prompt. Do NOT repeat targets already injected
> via `{WORD_TARGET}`, `{IMMERSION_RULE}`, `{PEDAGOGICAL_CONSTRAINTS}`, etc.
> Only add level-specific knowledge that the prompt template cannot provide.

## Grammar Scope

**Allowed:** Nominative, Accusative (from M25), Locative (from M13), Genitive (basics), Vocative.
Present tense verbs (from M15). Past tense (from M36). Future (from M37). Imperative (M47).

**Forbidden (unless plan exempts):** Dative case (A2), Instrumental case (A2), participles,
subordinate clauses (який, що-clause, коли, якщо, тому що, бо, щоб).

**Aspect:** Imperfective only — UNLESS the module's plan explicitly teaches perfective forms
(e.g., imperative module M47 uses perfective verbs like сказати, показати, взяти).

## Immersion Strategy (A1)

A1 immersion is achieved through **structural containment**:
- **Paragraphs** are English with Ukrainian vocabulary **bolded inline**
- **Full Ukrainian sentences** go in structural containers only: tables, bulleted example lists, blockquote dialogues, or pattern boxes
- **Vary containers** — never use the same type twice in a row
- Grammar rules and explanations stay in English at A1

The prompt injects a band-specific exemplar (`{TIER_EXEMPLAR}`) showing the exact pattern for the module's immersion level.

## Sentence Constraints

- Max 10 words per Ukrainian sentence (STRICT)

messages.content:
===CONTENT_START===
<!-- adapted from: Zaharijchuk, Grade 4, p.142 and Kravtsova, Grade 4, p.110 -->
Welcome to the pool! In Ukrainian, just like in your daily life, there are different ways to say "can." Sometimes you talk about a skill you've learned, sometimes about whether you're free to do something right now, and sometimes about whether you're allowed to do it. Today, you'll learn how to navigate these three situations with confidence.

## Могти (Can/Be able to)

The verb **могти** is one of the most common words in Ukrainian. It describes physical ability or circumstantial possibility—whether something is possible right now or in a specific situation.

One thing to watch out for is the stem change. Notice how the **г** in the infinitive **могти** changes to a **ж** in most forms, except for the "they" form.

| Person | Conjugation | English |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Я** | **можу** | I can |
| **Ти** | **можеш** | You can (informal) |
| **Він / Вона / Воно** | **може** | He / She / It can |
| **Ми** | **можемо** | We can |
| **Ви** | **можете** | You can (formal/plural) |
| **Вони** | **можуть** | They can |

Use **могти** when you want to say you are free to do something or have the physical capacity right now. 

> — Ти **можеш** прийти завтра? (Can you come tomorrow?)
> — Так, я **можу** прийти о п'ятій. (Yes, I can come at five.)
> — Я не **можу** зараз розмовляти. (I cannot talk right now.)

You can also use it for polite requests, similar to "May I...?" in English:

- **Можу** я запитати? (May I ask?)
- **Можу** я подивитися? (May I look?)
- **Можу** я увійти? (May I enter?)

## Вміти (Know how to)

While **могти** is about circumstances, **вміти** is all about acquired skills and talents. If you've learned how to do something—like drive, cook, or speak a language—you use **вміти**.

This verb follows a regular pattern for verbs ending in **-іти**, where the first-person singular ends in **-ію**.

| Person | Conjugation | English |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Я** | **вмію** | I know how to |
| **Ти** | **вмієш** | You know how to |
| **Він / Вона / Воно** | **вміє** | He / She / It knows how to |
| **Ми** | **вміємо** | We know how to |
| **Ви** | **вмієте** | You know how to |
| **Вони** | **вміють** | They know how to |

Think of **вміти** as the "skill verb." Here are some classic examples:

- Я **вмію** **плавати**. (I know how to swim.)
- Ти **вмієш** **готувати**? (Do you know how to cook?)
- Мій брат **вміє** **водити** машину. (My brother knows how to drive a car.)
- Вона дуже добре **вміє** **співати**. (She knows how to sing very well.)

The contrast is key. If you say «Я **вмію** плавати», it means you have the skill. If you say «Я **можу** плавати», it might mean the pool is open and you are free to go in!

## Можна / Не можна (It is allowed / Not allowed)

When you see a sign or ask for permission, you'll use the impersonal word **можна**. It never changes its form—no conjugation needed!

- **Можна** — It is allowed / May I?
- **Не можна** — It is not allowed / Prohibited

This is very practical for navigating public spaces in Ukraine. You'll see these words on signs everywhere.

> — Тут **можна** **фотографувати**? (Is it allowed to photograph here?)
> — Ні, тут **не можна**. (No, it's not allowed here.)

Use **можна** with an infinitive to ask for permission politely. It's often more common than **можу я** in daily situations.

- **Можна** увійти? (May I enter?)
- **Можна** подивитися меню? (May I look at the menu?)
- Тут **можна** їсти? (Is it allowed to eat here?)

You will also see **не можна** on prohibition signs:

- **Не можна** **курити**. (No smoking.)
- **Не можна** паркуватися. (No parking.)
- **Не можна** голосно розмовляти. (No loud talking.)

## Не можу vs Не вмію vs Не можна (Contrasting Negations)

Choosing the right way to say "cannot" depends on *why* you can't do it. Mixing these up is a common mistake for learners, but the distinction is simple once you see the logic.

1. **Не можу** (Circumstance): "I am unable right now." Maybe you're busy, tired, or the door is locked.
2. **Не вмію** (Knowledge): "I never learned." You lack the skill.
3. **Не можна** (Rule): "It's against the rules." Someone else said no.

Compare these situations:

- Я **не можу** прийти сьогодні. (I am busy or something happened—circumstance.)
- Я **не вмію** **кататися** на ковзанах. (I never learned how to skate—permanent gap.)
- Тут **не можна** **курити**. (It's a library or a hospital—external rule.)

Another example:
- Я **не вмію** **малювати**. (I'm not an artist.)
- Я **не можу** **малювати** зараз. (I don't have any paper or my hand hurts.)
- Тут **не можна** **малювати**. (It's a wall in a museum!)

Remember: If someone asks "Can you swim?", they might mean "Do you have the skill?" (**Ти вмієш?**) or "Are you able to go to the beach now?" (**Ти можеш?**).

## Практика (Practice)

Let's look at how these words work in real-life conversations. Notice which modal is chosen based on the context.

**У школі (At school)**
> — **Можна** запитати? (May I ask?)
> — Так, звичайно. (Yes, of course.)
> — Я **не можу** прочитати це слово. (I cannot read this word—circumstance/difficulty.)
> — Ти **вмієш** читати по-українськи? (Do you know how to read in Ukrainian?—skill.)

**У спортзалі (At the gym)**
> — Ти **вмієш** **грати** на гітарі? (Do you know how to play the guitar?)
> — Так, але я **не можу** **грати** сьогодні. (Yes, but I cannot play today.)
> — Чому? (Why?)
> — Тут **не можна** голосно **грати**. (Loud playing is not allowed here.)

**У кафе (At the cafe)**
> — Ви **можете** принести рахунок? (Can you bring the bill?)
> — Так, звичайно. (Yes, of course.)
> — Тут **можна** платити карткою? (Is it allowed to pay by card here?)
> — Так, **можна**. (Yes, it is.)

## Підсумок

In this module, you've mastered the three faces of "can" in Ukrainian:
1. **Могти** for circumstantial or physical ability (I can come).
2. **Вміти** for learned skills and talents (I know how to swim).
3. **Можна** for permission and rules (May I? / It's allowed).

**Self-check questions:**
1. How does the stem of **могти** change when you conjugate it?
2. Which verb would you use to say you know how to drive a car?
3. What is the difference between «Я **не можу** готувати» and «Я **не вмію** готувати»?
4. How do you ask "May I enter?" using **можна**?

===CONTENT_END===

Вміти (Know how to): 268
Можна / Не можна (It is allowed / Not allowed): 272
Не можу vs Не вмію vs Не можна (Contrasting Negations): 245
Практика (Practice): 210

Total: 1475
